cards.text: The comma operator {{c1::,}} evaluates the {{c2::left}} operand first, then the {{c3::right}} operand, and returns the value of the {{c4::right}} operand.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: The comma operator evaluates ++x (x becomes 2), then ++y (y becomes 3), and returns the result of the right operand (3).

cards.front: What is the precedence level of the comma operator?
cards.back: The comma operator has the lowest precedence of all operators, even lower than assignment.

cards.question: What's the difference between these two lines?
cards.answer: First: z gets value of b. Second: z gets value of a, b is evaluated and discarded.
cards.explanation: First evaluates as z = (a, b) due to parentheses. Second evaluates as (z = a), b due to comma's low precedence.

cards.text: Best practice: {{c1::Avoid}} using the comma operator, except within {{c2::for loops}}.

cards.front: What is the difference between the comma operator and separator commas?
cards.back: The comma operator (,) evaluates expressions sequentially. Separator commas are used in function calls, declarations, etc., and don't invoke the comma operator.

cards.question: Are these separator commas or comma operators?
cards.explanation: In function parameters and function call arguments, commas are separators, not comma operators.

cards.text: In the expression (a, b, c), the comma operator returns the value of {{c1::c}} (the rightmost operand).

cards.concept: Why the comma operator is rarely used
cards.explanation: The comma operator makes code harder to read and understand. Almost any use of the comma operator can be better written as separate statements. The only common exception is in for loop initialization/increment.
examples: for (int i=0, j=10; i<j; ++i, --j) // OK: common in for loops

cards.front: Why is the low precedence of the comma operator dangerous?
cards.back: Because it can cause unexpected grouping. For example, z = a, b evaluates as (z = a), b, not z = (a, b), which may not be what you intended.

cards.text: The comma operator has the {{c1::lowest}} precedence of all operators, so z = a, b evaluates as {{c2::(z = a), b}}, not z = (a, b).
Comma operator evaluates {{c1::both}} operands from {{c2::left}} to {{c3::right}}, returns value of {{c4::right}} operand.
